Article title: 16 people died in a blast at a Tennessee explosives factory early Friday, sheriff says
CENTER: An explosion at the Accurate Energetic Systems plant in rural Tennessee on Friday, October 10, 2025, killed 16 people, with authorities confirming no survivors. The blast leveled the facility, located approximately 60 miles southwest of Nashville, and was felt for miles around. The Accurate Energetic Systems plant supplies and researches explosives for the military. The cause of the explosion remains unknown, and investigators are conducting a slow, methodical search due to the presence of volatile material. Around 300 responders, including a "rapid DNA" team, are on site, and officials state it could take weeks or months to rule out foul play. The community is grieving, holding vigils for the victims and their families.

Article title: Afghanistan kills dozens of Pakistani soldiers in cross-border attack, Taliban government says
LEFT: Intense cross-border clashes erupted along the Pakistan-Afghanistan border on Saturday night, following an attack by Afghan Taliban forces on Pakistani military posts. This escalation occurred after Afghanistan accused Pakistan of conducting airstrikes in Kabul earlier in the week. Pakistan responded with retaliatory strikes and ground raids on Sunday. Pakistan's military reported 23 soldiers killed and 29 wounded, while the Afghan Taliban spokesperson claimed 58 Pakistani soldiers were killed. Pakistan accuses Afghanistan of harboring Tehreek-e-Taliban Pakistan (TTP) militants responsible for escalating attacks on Pakistani soil, a claim denied by Afghanistan's foreign minister.
CENTER: Cross-border attacks between Afghanistan and Pakistan have led to conflicting casualty reports and heightened tensions. The Taliban government claimed its forces killed 58 Pakistani military personnel and wounded 30 in retaliation for alleged Pakistani airspace violations and bombing a market. Pakistan, however, reported 23 to 29 of its soldiers died and 29 were injured, asserting it neutralized over 200 Taliban and affiliated fighters. Islamabad accused Kabul of harboring terrorists targeting Pakistan, a claim the Taliban denied. The clashes resulted in the closure of the Torkham and Chaman border crossings, while Saudi Arabia and Qatar called for de-escalation and dialogue.

Article title: Diane Keaton, Oscar-winning star of 'Annie Hall' and 'The Godfather,' dies at 79
RIGHT: Actress Diane Keaton, celebrated for her Oscar-winning role in 'Annie Hall' and her performances in 'The Godfather' films, died on October 11 at the age of 79. Her family confirmed her passing to People magazine. Friends and colleagues reported a rapid decline in her health during her final months, noting significant weight loss. Keaton, who also received the AFI Lifetime Achievement Award in 2017, was remembered by numerous co-stars, including Steve Martin, Goldie Hawn, and Bette Midler, for her talent and unique spirit. She is survived by her adopted children, Dexter and Duke.
CENTER: Oscar-winning actress Diane Keaton, known for her iconic roles in films such as "Annie Hall" and "The Godfather" trilogy, has died at 79. Her death was confirmed by producer Dori Rath to NPR and People Magazine. Celebrated for her quirky, vibrant manner and unique style, Keaton won her first Academy Award for Best Actress in 1978 for "Annie Hall." She received three additional Oscar nominations during her career for "Reds," "Marvin's Room," and "Something's Gotta Give." Born Diane Hall in Los Angeles in 1946, Keaton never married but adopted two children, Dexter and Duke, in her 50s.

Article title: Golf Legend Phil Mickelson: Charlie Kirk Being Awarded Presidential Medal of Freedom Is 'Well Deserved'
RIGHT: President Donald Trump plans to posthumously award Turning Point USA founder Charlie Kirk the Presidential Medal of Freedom, the nation's highest civilian honor. The White House ceremony is scheduled to coincide with what would have been Kirk's 32nd birthday. Trump first announced his intention on September 11, calling Kirk "a champion of liberty." Kirk, 31, was fatally shot on September 10 while speaking at Utah Valley University. Golf legend Phil Mickelson commented on the announcement, stating, "This award is well deserved," and expressed his wish that Kirk were still alive.

Article title: Madagascar’s president says illegal power grab by military is under way
LEFT: Madagascar's President Andry Rajoelina stated an "attempt to seize power illegally and by force" was underway after the elite Capsat military unit joined youth-led protests and announced it was taking over the army. The protests, which began on September 25 over water and electricity shortages, had expanded to demand Rajoelina's resignation. On Saturday, Capsat soldiers joined demonstrators in Antananarivo. By Sunday, General Demosthene Pikulas, head of Capsat, was installed as chief of the army staff. Prime Minister Ruphin Fortunat Zafisambo had called for dialogue, while President Rajoelina affirmed he was "managing national affairs." Capsat had previously brought Rajoelina to power in a 2009 coup.
CENTER: Madagascar's President Andry Rajoelina's office announced an illegal attempt to seize power was underway, condemning efforts to destabilize the country. This followed an army unit, CAPSAT, claiming control of all armed forces and appointing Gen Demosthene Pikulas as the new army chief of staff, an appointment reportedly accepted by the armed forces minister. Protests, initially over utility cuts and escalating due to wider dissatisfaction with Rajoelina's government, gained momentum after CAPSAT troops joined demonstrators, who subsequently occupied the key May 13 Square in Antananarivo. Reports of a shoot-out at a CAPSAT camp resulted in one soldier's death.

Article title: Militia strikes kill at least 60 in Sudan displacement camp, says El Fasher group
LEFT: Militia drone and artillery strikes by the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) have killed at least 60 people at the Dar al-Arqam displacement shelter in El Fasher, western Sudan. The El Fasher resistance committee reported the shelter, located on university grounds, was hit twice by drones and eight times by artillery shells over Friday and Saturday. The Sudan Doctors’ Network reported 53 killed, including 14 children and 15 women. El Fasher is the last state capital in Darfur not controlled by the RSF, which has besieged the city for over 500 days. The UN High Commissioner for Human Rights condemned the RSF's actions.
CENTER: A shelling and drone attack by the Sudanese Rapid Support Forces (RSF) hit a shelter for displaced families in El Fasher, Darfur, killing at least 53 people on Friday. The Sudan Doctors’ Network reported that among the dead were 14 children and 15 women, with 21 others wounded, many seriously. The attack targeted al-Arqam Home, a shelter located at Omdurman Islamic University. This incident is the latest in Sudan's ongoing conflict, which began in April 2023. El Fasher, the provincial capital of North Darfur and the military's last stronghold in Darfur, has been under an RSF blockade and subjected to shelling for months.

Article title: Minister says Starmer security adviser had no role in China spy case evidence
LEFT: Education Secretary Bridget Phillipson stated that Jonathan Powell, the government's national security adviser, had no involvement in the decision to drop the prosecution against two British men accused of spying for China. Phillipson affirmed Powell was not connected to discussions about the case's substance or evidence, and Keir Starmer retains full confidence in him. The prosecution against Christopher Cash and Christopher Berry was abandoned in September. Stephen Parkinson, Director of Public Prosecutions, indicated the case was dropped because ministers declined to state China posed a national security threat, which was deemed necessary for a successful prosecution under the Official Secrets Act. The UK’s updated national security strategy classifies China as a 'geostrategic challenge'.
CENTER: Education Secretary Bridget Phillipson asserted that Jonathan Powell, Labour leader Keir Starmer's national security adviser, had no involvement in the "substance or the evidence" of the collapsed China spy case. This statement came amid Conservative accusations that Powell withheld crucial evidence, leading to charges against Christopher Cash and Christopher Berry being dropped. The Director of Public Prosecutions, Stephen Parkinson, clarified that the trial failed because the government did not provide evidence designating China as a national security threat. Former Conservative ministers, however, claim a document with "hundreds" of examples of Chinese threats existed during the alleged offence period (December 2021-February 2023).

Article title: Trump directs Pentagon to use ‘all available funds’ to pay troops despite shutdown
RIGHT: President Donald Trump has directed Secretary of War Pete Hegseth to utilize "all available funds" to ensure U.S. service members receive their paychecks by October 15th, amidst an ongoing government shutdown. The shutdown, which began on October 1st, has entered its second week, causing financial strain for military personnel and federal workers. Trump criticized Senate Democrats, particularly Leader Chuck Schumer, for blocking funding resolutions and holding the military "hostage." Republicans, including Speaker Mike Johnson, have also blamed Democrats for the impasse. Financial institutions have activated bridge loan programs, and nearly 35,000 letters have been sent to Congress advocating for the Pay Our Troops Act.
CENTER: President Donald Trump announced he has directed the Defense Department to use “all available funds” to ensure U.S. troops receive their paychecks on October 15th, despite the ongoing government shutdown. This directive, issued on Saturday, aims to provide a short-term fix for the approximately 1.3 million active-duty service members. The White House budget office indicated that Pentagon research and development funds would be utilized for this purpose. Trump blamed Democrats for the shutdown, which entered its 11th day, but this action does not extend to other furloughed federal workers. It remains unclear if the directive applies to the U.S. Coast Guard.

Article title: Trump threatens 100% China tariffs as Beijing restricts rare-earth exports
LEFT: Donald Trump threatened to impose 100% additional tariffs on Chinese imports, along with new controls on critical software, effective November 1st or sooner. This followed his accusation that China was making "very hostile" moves to restrict rare-earth exports. China's commerce ministry stated it would take "resolute measures" if the US escalated, while insisting its rare-earth export controls were "not export bans" and for "civil use." US financial markets reacted sharply, with approximately $2 trillion wiped off US stocks. China is the world's largest producer, supplying over 90% of processed rare earths.
CENTER: Tensions between the US and China have escalated following President Trump's threat of an additional 100% tariff on Chinese goods. Trump accused China of "becoming very hostile" and attempting to hold the world "captive" by tightening rare-earth export controls. China, which processes approximately 90% of the world's rare earths vital for advanced technology, called the tariff threat "US double standards" and asserted it is "not afraid" of a trade war, vowing countermeasures. Beijing defended its export restrictions as national security measures. The dispute has renewed fears of a trade war, rattled financial markets, and cast doubt on a potential meeting between Trump and President Xi Jinping.

Article title: What we know about the Gaza ceasefire agreement
LEFT: A ceasefire agreement is in effect in Gaza, initiating a swap of Israeli hostages for Palestinian prisoners. Hamas is expected to release living Israeli hostages by Monday noon local time, after which Israel will release nearly 2,000 Palestinian detainees. This exchange represents the first phase of a broader US-brokered peace plan, which includes US President Donald Trump visiting the region and co-chairing a summit aimed at finalizing a permanent truce. Concurrently, humanitarian aid, with approximately 600 trucks expected daily, is set to enter Gaza to restore pre-war supply levels. The US will deploy up to 200 troops in Israel to assist stabilization efforts, though not directly within Gaza.
RIGHT: An MSNBC host, Jonathan Capehart, stated on "PBS NewsHour" that the Israel-Hamas deal was achieved due to President Donald Trump's focused efforts on securing the release of hostages. The initial phase of the agreement is anticipated to involve the release of 20 Israeli hostages, presumed alive, in exchange for 2,000 Palestinians held by Israel. Capehart expressed skepticism regarding the deal's long-term viability beyond this first phase, citing unresolved challenges such as the disarmament of Hamas, the establishment of a legitimate Palestinian negotiating partner, and the potential for Palestinian self-governance or a two-state solution, which Prime Minister Netanyahu has reportedly rejected.
CENTER: A Gaza ceasefire took effect Friday morning, brokered by US President Donald Trump, marking the first phase of a broader agreement. Under this initial phase, Hamas is expected to release 20 living Israeli hostages and the remains of up to 28 deceased hostages by Monday 12:00 local time. In exchange, Israel will release approximately 250 Palestinian prisoners and 1,700 detainees from Gaza. The deal also includes increased humanitarian aid for Gaza, with an aim for 600 trucks daily, and a partial withdrawal of Israeli troops from parts of the Strip. An international peace summit, co-chaired by Trump and Egypt's President al-Sisi, will convene in Sharm El-Sheikh on Monday with over 20 leaders to sign the peace plan and discuss regional stability. A US-led multinational force will monitor the ceasefire.
